title: What is a Promise?
instruction:
A Promise represents a value that doesn't exist yet — but will in the future (or won't, if something goes wrong).

A Promise has three states:
- **Pending** — initial state, waiting
- **Fulfilled** — succeeded, has a value
- **Rejected** — failed, has a reason

Run this to see a Promise resolve.
expectedOutput: Fulfilled with: I have a value!
successMessage: A Promise is an object. resolve() puts it in the fulfilled state. .then() runs when the Promise fulfils — not immediately, but in the next microtask. More on this in Chapter 20.

title: Simulating async work
instruction:
In the real world, Promises wrap async operations (network calls, file reads). We'll simulate them with `setTimeout`.

Notice how the code after `fetchUser()` runs BEFORE the result arrives — that's the whole point of async.
expectedOutput: This runs before the user arrives!
successMessage: The last line printed SECOND — even though it appears after the .then. JavaScript scheduled the setTimeout and moved on. That's the async model.

title: .catch — handle rejection
instruction:
`.catch(fn)` handles a rejected Promise. Always add it — unhandled rejections crash Node.js.

`.catch(fn)` is shorthand for `.then(undefined, fn)`.
successMessage: .catch is your safety net. Without it, a rejected Promise is 'unhandled' — Node.js throws an UnhandledPromiseRejection and may crash your server.

title: .then chaining
instruction:
Each `.then()` returns a new Promise — so you can chain them.

The value returned from one `.then` becomes the input to the next.

Transform data through a chain: fetch → extract name → uppercase → log.
successMessage: Each .then receives the return value of the previous one. The chain flows top to bottom. An error at any step jumps straight to .catch — skipping remaining .thens.

title: .finally — always runs
instruction:
`.finally()` runs whether the Promise fulfilled or rejected — just like `finally` in try/catch.

Perfect for hiding loading spinners, closing connections.
successMessage: .finally always runs — success or failure. This is how loading spinners are hidden, how database connections are closed, how cleanup always happens.

title: Promise.all — run in parallel
instruction:
`Promise.all([p1, p2, p3])` runs all Promises in parallel and waits for ALL to complete.

If any one fails, the whole thing fails. One bad apple rejects the group.

Run three 'requests' in parallel.
successMessage: Three requests in ~150-200ms, not 330ms (sequential). Promise.all runs them in parallel — complete when the SLOWEST finishes. This is how APIs fetch multiple resources efficiently.

title: Promise.allSettled — get all results
instruction:
`Promise.allSettled` waits for all Promises and gives you all results — even the failed ones.

Unlike `Promise.all`, one failure doesn't cancel the rest.

Get results from all requests, successful or not.
successMessage: allSettled never rejects — it always resolves with an array of { status, value/reason } objects. Use it when you want results from all requests regardless of failures.

title: Promise.race — first one wins
instruction:
`Promise.race([p1, p2, p3])` resolves or rejects as soon as the FIRST Promise settles.

Perfect for timeouts: race a real request against a timer that rejects after N milliseconds.
hint: const timeout = new Promise((_, reject) => setTimeout(() => reject(new Error('Timeout!')), 500));
successMessage: withTimeout is a standard pattern. The real request races against a timer — whoever settles first wins. This is how you prevent requests from hanging forever.

title: Build a retry mechanism
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Write `retry(fn, maxAttempts, delayMs)` that:
- Calls `fn()` (which returns a Promise)
- If it rejects, waits `delayMs` ms then tries again
- After `maxAttempts` failures, rejects with the last error
- Logs attempt number before each try

Test with a function that fails the first 2 times then succeeds.
hint: async function retry(fn, attempts, delay) { for (let i = 1; i <= attempts; i++) { try { return await fn(); } catch(e) { if (i === attempts) throw e; await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, delay)); } } }

codeChecks.message: Use a loop for multiple attempts.
Use setTimeout or Promise for delay.

successMessage: A retry function — used in every production system for network requests, database calls, and external APIs. Chapter 17 done — Promises mastered!
